one_line_takeaway: RLCracker is a black-box, detector-free RL attack requiring only 100 short watermarked samples that drives evasion success up to 98.5% against Unigram (vs. 6.75% for GPT-4o paraphrasing) while preserving 0.92 semantic similarity, exposing that current LLM watermarks are far less robust under adaptive attacks (system prompts, reasoning-guided rewriting, RL policy optimization) than average-case evaluations suggest.
key_finding: On the 1500-token Unigram watermark, RLCracker with a Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct attacker (trained on only 100 short samples) achieves 98.5% ESR versus GPT-4o's 6.75% ESR -- a >90-point gap -- while maintaining P-SP semantic similarity of 0.92 (Table 5), compared to SIRA's 88% raw removal rate but only 0.47 P-SP (i.e., SIRA removes watermarks but destroys semantic fidelity on long text). The paper also shows two overlooked adversarial levers: (i) adding a role-assignment system prompt alone raises ESR substantially, e.g. Qwen3-4B on EWD-500 from 39.3% (Base) to 54.3% (SysP.), and KGW selfhash from 70.0% to 79.3%; (ii) enabling paraphraser reasoning ('Think') raises Qwen3-4B ESR on EWD-500 from 39.3% to 76.0%, and on 1500-token KGW selfhash from 36.0% to 78.0%. RLCracker generalizes cross-model (OOD): a 0.6B attacker trained on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct outputs reaches 96% ESR against Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct-generated EWD watermarks vs. 12.5% for Base (Table 2), and needs as few as 50 training samples of 250 tokens to reach 82.5% ESR (Table 3), with only minor degradation (~3% ESR drop at worst) when trained on mixed watermark-key data (Table 4).
core_contribution: The paper (ICML 2026, Shanghai Jiao Tong Univ. et al.) introduces two things as its own contribution: (1) the 'adaptive robustness radius' r*(X), a formal, certified-robustness-style metric quantifying the worst-case semantic margin within which an LLM watermark remains detectable under adaptive paraphrase attacks, plus a tractable relaxation ('KL adaptive radius') obtained by lifting the paraphrase space into a KL-divergence ball around a local watermark distribution, together with a theorem (4.3) giving a computable lower-bound certificate rho* derived under a sub-Gaussian detector-score assumption; and (2) RLCracker, a reinforcement-learning-based (GRPO-derived) watermark-removal attack that trains a paraphrasing policy using only question-watermarked-response pairs and zero access to the watermark detector, optimizing a combined objective of semantic-fidelity reward (P-SP score), token-wise KL reward pushing outputs away from an approximated watermark distribution Pwm and toward a human-like distribution Ph (both approximated via a reference model conditioned on the watermarked text vs. the original question), a KL regularizer to the reference policy, and a perplexity penalty for fluency.
threat_model: Black-box, detector-free adversary defined explicitly in Section 3: the attacker does not know the watermarking algorithm's internal design, the secret key s, the detector function f, or threshold delta, but may query the generator to observe watermarked outputs. Capability: collect question-watermarked sample pairs, select/tune a paraphrasing model, adjust its parameters, and choose any conditioning context (including system prompts), with no detector access. Success is defined as achieving low semantic distance (P-SP score > 0.7) to the original AND evading detection (detector score below threshold).
limitations: The paper's own stated caveats: the KL adaptive radius is a relaxation/lower-bound certificate, not the true instance-level worst-case radius, since direct computation of r*(X) is intractable; the sub-Gaussian detector-score assumption is 'mild' but is an assumption, not universally verified per-scheme; the certificate rho* relies on detector statistics (mu, sigma^2) that are available to the watermark designer but not to external evaluators. Empirically, some watermark schemes (e.g., Unigram, KGW) show much lower ESR than others even under RLCracker (e.g., KGW selfhash on 1500 tokens stays around 48-64% for smaller attackers), indicating resistance is scheme- and setting-dependent rather than uniformly broken. The paper does not claim to defeat all watermarking approaches equally and does not evaluate against watermark schemes with active detector-hardening countermeasures.

quantitative_results: RLCracker (Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct) ESR 98.5% vs GPT-4o ESR 6.75% on 1500-token Unigram watermark
RLCracker P-SP score 0.92 vs SIRA P-SP score 0.47 on 1500-token Unigram (Table 5)
SIRA raw removal rate 88% but near-zero ESR on long-form text due to low semantic fidelity
System prompt (SysP.) raises Qwen3-4B ESR from 39.3% to 54.3% on EWD-500, and from 70.0% to 79.3% on KGW selfhash-500
Reasoning-guided paraphrase (Think) raises Qwen3-4B ESR from 39.3% to 76.0% on EWD-500, and from 36.0% to 78.0% on KGW selfhash-1500
Cross-model OOD: 0.6B attacker trained on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct outputs reaches 96% ESR vs Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct-generated EWD watermarks, vs 12.5% for Base (Table 2)
Training-size ablation: 50 samples x 250 tokens yields 82.5% ESR for Qwen3-0.6B on EWD; plateaus beyond ~100 samples (Table 3)
Mixed watermark-key training causes at most ~3% ESR drop (largest for EWD on 0.6B model) (Table 4)
Pass@20 multi-sample attack on EWD reaches 89% success at 0.7 semantic threshold vs 32% Pass@1 baseline (Figure 2)
baselines_compared: SIRA (self-information rewrite attack, Cheng et al. 2025)
